us-politics.headline: Trump's Iran Threats Divide Congress, Raise War Crime Concerns
us-politics.body:
President Trump's Easter message threatening Iran has drawn sharp criticism from both parties. Sen. Chris Murphy called the threat a 'clear war crime,' while Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene broke with Trump, calling the posture 'insane.' Rep. Adam Smith, the top Armed Services Democrat, warned a war with Iran would be a costly mistake.

The debate sharpened after Iran claimed a US aircraft was destroyed during a recovery mission for a missing service member - a mission Netanyahu publicly praised Trump for. A House Republican said he does not expect ground troops will be needed to open the Strait of Hormuz, signaling some GOP members are trying to contain the escalation. Rep. Madeleine Dean also raised concerns about the budget implications of any military confrontation.

sources.title: Chris Murphy on Trump’s Iran threat: ‘A clear war crime’
Iran says US aircraft destroyed during recovery mission for missing service member
Rep. Madeleine Dean discusses Iran and President Trump's proposed budget
House Republican says he doesn’t think ground troops will be needed to open the Strait of Hormuz
Marjorie Taylor Greene rails against ‘insane’ Trump after Easter Iran threat
Top Armed Services Democrat says Iran war ‘mistake in large part because of the cost’
Netanyahu says he spoke with Trump, congratulated him on rescuing downed pilot

us-politics.headline: Trump Fires Bondi and Noem, Rules Out Further Cabinet Shake-Up
us-politics.body:
Trump dismissed Attorney General Pam Bondi and Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem, but told reporters not to expect further Cabinet changes. The departures came as Republican divisions and Trump's own detachment have stalled GOP efforts to reopen the Department of Homeland Security following a partial shutdown.

Saturday Night Live mocked Bondi's exit in its cold open, reflecting the broader public attention the firings attracted. The DHS situation remains unresolved, with House and Senate Republicans struggling to align on a path forward.

sources.title: SNL rips Bondi’s departure in cold open
Republican divisions, Trump’s detachment stymie GOP efforts to reopen DHS
Trump says to not expect Cabinet shake-up after Bondi, Noem firings

us-politics.headline: Trump's Mail-In Voting Order and Cuba Policy Draw Democratic Fire
us-politics.body:
Stacey Abrams called Trump's executive order targeting mail-in voting 'patently illegal,' arguing it oversteps presidential authority and threatens ballot access. The order is likely to face legal challenges.

Separately, two Democratic lawmakers returning from Cuba slammed US policy as a 'blockade of fuel' and 'economic bombing' of the island. The visit puts them at odds with the administration's hardline stance and with many in their own party who remain cautious about appearing sympathetic to the Cuban government.

sources.title: Pair of Democrat lawmakers slam 'blockade of fuel' to Cuba, 'economic bombing' after visit to island
Stacey Abrams on Trump’s mail-in voting order: ‘Patently illegal’

us-politics.headline: Democrats Eye 2028, Debate Strategy for Reaching New Voters
us-politics.body:
Arizona Rep. Ruben Gallego said he is open to a 2028 presidential run, telling reporters 'we have to look at it.' His profile rose after flipping a Senate seat in a competitive state in 2024.

Rep. Ro Khanna argued Democrats who resist engaging with unconventional media figures like streamer Hasan Piker are making a strategic error, saying that approach 'will cost us future elections.' The exchange reflects a live internal debate over how the party rebuilds its coalition ahead of the next cycle.

sources.title: Khanna: Those saying ‘don’t engage’ with personalities like Hasan Piker ‘will cost us future elections’
Gallego on possible 2028 presidential run: ‘We have to look at it’

au-politics.headline: Fuel Crisis Eases But Pressure Mounts on Energy Security
au-politics.body:
Australia has secured fuel shipments through well into May, offering short-term relief after weeks of supply anxiety. Petrol prices have dropped 32 cents over the past week following fuel tax cuts, according to live national data. The auction clearance rate has also hit its lowest point since 2022, suggesting broader economic pressure on households.

The crisis is reshaping consumer behaviour. Used electric vehicle prices are rising sharply as drivers ditch combustion engines, with one seller telling the Guardian it no longer makes sense to hold onto petrol cars. The Spectator, from the right, argues the government has failed where the US succeeded - by not guaranteeing domestic oil supply - and calls the current situation a foreseeable policy failure.

sources.title: Australia news live: national auction clearance rate hits lowest point since 2022; petrol drops 32 cents over past week after fuel tax cuts
Used EV prices rise as Australia’s fuel crisis hits: ‘Doesn’t make sense to hold on to a combustion engine’
The US guaranteed its oil, why can’t Australia?
Australia's fuel shipments secured 'well into' May

au-politics.headline: Deeming Wins Preselection as Liberal Party Tensions Simmer
au-politics.body:
Moira Deeming has won her Victorian Liberal preselection battle, surviving a protracted internal dispute that exposed deep divisions within the state party. Deeming, a conservative MP previously suspended by then-leader John Pesutto over her attendance at a gender-critical rally, now holds her spot heading into the next election cycle.

The result lands as the Liberals face broader strategic questions. The Spectator, arguing from the right, criticises the party for supporting social media censorship laws, calling it a betrayal of liberal principles that will cost them with younger voters. The preselection outcome and the censorship debate together point to an opposition still searching for a coherent identity after its federal election loss.

sources.title: Liberals are fools to support social media censorship
Moira Deeming wins Victorian Liberal preselection battle

au-politics.headline: Human Rights Commission Probes Racial Discrimination by NSW Government
au-politics.body:
The Australian Human Rights Commission is investigating a racial discrimination complaint against the NSW government, with details of the complaint emerging ahead of the Easter long weekend. The case centres on alleged discriminatory conduct by a state body, though the specific agency and complainant have not been publicly named.

The investigation adds to existing scrutiny of state-level race relations policy. NSW has faced recurring criticism over the treatment of Aboriginal communities in areas including housing, policing and child protection. A formal commission inquiry carries legal weight and can compel responses from government, making the outcome significant regardless of findings.

sources.title: Australian Human Rights Commission investigates racial discrimination complaint against NSW government

au-politics.headline: Victoria's Free Train Tickets Cause Long Weekend Chaos
au-politics.body:
Victoria's free public transport offer over the Easter long weekend overwhelmed regional train services, leaving passengers standing for hours on routes not built for the surge in demand. The state government promoted the initiative as a cost-of-living measure, but the rollout exposed a mismatch between the policy and the capacity of regional rail infrastructure.

The scenes drew criticism that the government failed to plan for predictable demand spikes. Supporters of the scheme argue the overcrowding reflects its success in getting people out of cars, and that infrastructure investment should follow. The tension between affordability policy and service delivery is likely to follow the government into budget season.

sources.title: Passengers forced to stand for hours on regional Victoria trains as free tickets supercharge long weekend demand

world.headline: Iran War Escalates as Hormuz Standoff Threatens Global Shipping
world.body:
Tehran has rejected Trump's deadline to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, keeping one of the world's most critical oil chokepoints under threat. Two people were killed in Haifa after an Iranian missile struck a residential building, while BBC reporters near the strait described conditions at the edge of an active battlefield. Trump declared victory following the rescue of a downed US airman from a remote part of Iran, but officials warn the broader operation remains exposed.

The conflict is already rippling outward. Vietnam's gig workers are absorbing sharp fuel cost increases driven by the disruption to global oil markets. Iran's internet shutdown has now broken records for wartime duration, deepening isolation for ordinary Iranians. In the UK, seven protesters were arrested near an RAF base being used by US forces.

sources.title: Vietnam’s gig workers slammed by rising fuel costs amid fallout of Iran war
Trump declares victory after rescue but threats to US operation in Iran still loom
Iran war live: Tehran rejects Trump’s Hormuz deadline; 2 killed in Haifa
'We are at the edge of a battlefield': BBC reports near Strait of Hormuz
How rescue of US airman in remote part of Iran unfolded
UK police arrest seven protesters near RAF base used by US
Trump threatens Iran in expletive-filled social media post
Frustration grows as Iran’s wartime internet shutdown breaks grim record
Video captures Iranian missile striking residential building in Haifa

world.headline: Gaza Bleeds On as Hamas Rejects Disarmament and Strikes Continue
world.body:
Hamas's armed wing has flatly rejected disarmament demands, calling them unacceptable while Israeli air strikes continue to kill civilians across Gaza. One strike hit a crowd in Gaza City. Christians in Gaza marked Easter without celebration, describing the holiday as hollow amid ongoing bombardment and what many are calling a genocide.

Conditions inside Gaza's tent camps are deteriorating further, with rodent infestations now spreading alongside the violence. Aid access remains severely constrained. Israel is simultaneously striking targets in Lebanon, where air strikes and shelling are adding to a rising death toll and displacing thousands more civilians.

sources.title: Has the humanitarian crisis in Gaza been ignored?
Hamas armed wing says disarmament demands not acceptable
Israeli air strike hits crowd in Gaza City
Hamas armed wing rejects disarmament while Israel continues attacks
Israel continues deadly attacks on Gaza while striking Iran, Lebanon
Rodent infestations and attacks spread in Gaza tent camps
‘Refuse to celebrate’: Christians in Gaza mark sombre Easter amid genocide
Israeli air strikes, shelling across Lebanon add to rising death toll
Displaced in Lebanon: 'Lives turned upside down'

world.headline: Explosives Found at Russian Gas Pipeline Spanning Serbia and Hungary
world.body:
Both Serbia and Hungary say explosives were discovered at a Russian gas pipeline running through their territory, with Hungary alleging a deliberate plot timed ahead of its upcoming election. The incident raises immediate concerns about energy infrastructure security in Central Europe, where dependence on Russian gas remains politically sensitive.

No group has claimed responsibility. The discovery comes as European governments are already on heightened alert over sabotage threats to critical infrastructure following earlier incidents in the Baltic Sea.

sources.title: Hungary alleges plot to blow up gas pipeline ahead of election
Serbia, Hungary say explosives found at Russian gas pipeline

world.headline: Nigeria Rescues 31 Hostages; Migrant Boat Capsizes Off Libya
world.body:
Nigeria's army rescued 31 hostages following an attack on a church, in one of the more significant counter-kidnapping operations the military has carried out in recent months. Details on the attackers and their affiliations remain limited.

Separately, dozens of migrants are missing after a boat capsized near Libya. The Mediterranean crossing continues to claim lives at a steady pace, with Libya remaining the primary departure point for people attempting to reach Europe.

sources.title: Nigeria's army rescues 31 hostages after church attack
Dozens missing after boat carrying migrants capsizes near Libya

ai.headline: Microsoft Labels Copilot 'Entertainment Only' in Terms of Service
ai.body:
Microsoft's terms of service now classify Copilot as being "for entertainment purposes only," a quiet legal disclaimer that raises serious questions about how much the company stands behind its flagship AI product. The language effectively shields Microsoft from liability if users rely on Copilot for professional, medical, legal, or financial decisions.

The framing is awkward for a tool Microsoft has aggressively marketed to enterprise customers and bundled into Office products. It signals a growing tension in the industry between selling AI as a productivity revolution and limiting legal exposure when it gets things wrong. Trust is the product. The fine print is the reality.

sources.title: Copilot is 'for entertainment purposes only', per Microsoft's terms of use
Copilot is ‘for entertainment purposes only,’ according to Microsoft’s terms of use

ai.headline: Google's Gemma 4 Runs Locally on iPhones and Laptops
ai.body:
Google's Gemma 4 model is now running on consumer hardware, with users demonstrating it on iPhones via the Google AI Edge Gallery app and on Apple Silicon laptops through LM Studio's new headless CLI. Separately, a developer built a real-time audio and video assistant using Gemma on an M3 Pro, with voice output running entirely on-device.

The push toward local inference is accelerating fast. A browser-embedded project called Gemma Gem also surfaced this week, letting users run the model with no API keys or cloud connection at all. Taken together, these projects suggest capable on-device AI is shifting from novelty to practical baseline.

sources.title: Gemma 4 on iPhone
Running Gemma 4 locally with LM Studio's new headless CLI and Claude Code
Show HN: Real-time AI (audio/video in, voice out) on an M3 Pro with Gemma E2B
Show HN: Gemma Gem – AI model embedded in a browser – no API keys, no cloud

ai.headline: Japan's Labor Shortage Is a Live Test for Physical AI
ai.body:
Japan is deploying robots not to displace workers but to fill roles that go unfilled - elder care, logistics, and manual labor in a country with a shrinking workforce and deep cultural resistance to immigration. The results are showing that physical AI can handle real-world conditions well enough to matter.

The contrast with Western AI anxiety is sharp. Where debates in the US and Europe center on job destruction, Japan is treating robotics as a demographic necessity. That framing may prove more durable, and more honest, about what automation is actually solving.

sources.title: In Japan, the robot isn't coming for your job; it's filling the one nobody wants
In Japan, the robot isn’t coming for your job; it’s filling the one nobody wants

ai.headline: Employers Mine Personal Data to Lowball Salary Offers
ai.body:
Employers are increasingly using personal data - drawn from social media, financial records, and third-party data brokers - to estimate the lowest salary a candidate will accept before making an offer. The practice turns the negotiation into an information asymmetry problem that workers rarely know they are losing.

The tactic is legal in most jurisdictions and largely invisible to job seekers. It mirrors how advertisers use behavioral data to set prices dynamically, applied now to labor markets. Workers negotiating blind against employers armed with their financial profiles are at a structural disadvantage that salary transparency laws only partially address.

sources.title: Employers use your personal data to figure out the lowest salary you'll accept

btc.headline: Bitcoin bounces to $69K but bear case still looms
btc.body:
Bitcoin clawed back above $69,000 Monday as ceasefire talks between warring parties eased geopolitical pressure and a wave of short liquidations amplified the move higher. Trump's delayed deadline on Iran added fuel, sending crypto markets broadly upward.

The rally has skeptics unconvinced. One analyst warned that without a decisive reclaim of $75,000, a collapse toward $10,000 remains the base case - pointing to weak macro support and fragile market structure beneath the bounce.

The tension between short-term relief and longer-term technical damage defines the moment. Bulls need follow-through. Without it, this looks like a dead-cat bounce in a still-broken trend.

sources.title: Bitcoin reclaims $69,000 as ceasefire talks surface and crypto shorts get squeezed
Bitcoin meltdown to $10,000 remains likely unless prices reclaim $75,000, analyst says
Trump-Iran deadline chaos sends crypto higher while cease-fire hopes rise

btc.headline: Geopolitics rattles crypto: Iran odds spike, Polymarket retreats
btc.body:
Prediction market Polymarket briefly showed 63% odds of a US invasion of Iran this year following a Trump social media post, reflecting how quickly crypto-native markets price in geopolitical risk. The spike drew immediate attention across financial and policy circles.

Polymarket then pulled a separate set of Iran-related markets - ones framed around rescue scenarios - after users and commentators pushed back hard on their framing. The episode exposed the fine line between legitimate risk markets and content that reads as exploitative during a live crisis.

Michael Saylor, meanwhile, hinted Strategy is preparing another Bitcoin purchase after a week-long pause, suggesting institutional buyers are watching the dip closely.

sources.title: Michael Saylor hints at next Strategy Bitcoin buy after weeklong pause
Polymarket odds of US invading Iran this year reach 63% after Trump's post
Polymarket pulls controversial Iran rescue markets after intense backlash

btc.headline: China bans Bitchat, Rwanda blocks Bybit - regulators tighten grip
btc.body:
Apple removed Jack Dorsey's Bitchat app from its China App Store at Beijing's request, the latest example of the Chinese government using platform pressure to control decentralized communication tools. Bitchat routes messages over Bitcoin's network, making it harder to censor - which apparently made it a target.

Separately, Rwanda moved to shut down Bybit's peer-to-peer platform that allowed franc-to-crypto trading. The move signals that African regulators, often assumed to be permissive toward crypto, are increasingly willing to act against unlicensed exchange activity.

Taken together, both cases show governments across very different contexts reaching for the same lever: cutting off the on-ramps.

sources.title: Apple pulls Jack Dorsey's Bitchat from China at Beijing’s request
Rwanda swats Bybit’s P2P platform offering franc-to-crypto trading

btc.headline: Quantum computing threat to Bitcoin is real - and growing
btc.body:
A new explainer from CoinDesk lays out why quantum computing poses a genuine long-term threat to Bitcoin's cryptographic foundations. Current quantum machines cannot break Bitcoin's encryption, but the trajectory of the technology suggests that window may not stay open indefinitely.

The core risk is that a sufficiently powerful quantum computer could derive private keys from public keys, allowing an attacker to drain wallets. Dormant coins with exposed public keys - including potentially Satoshi's early holdings - would be most vulnerable first.

The Bitcoin developer community is aware of the problem and post-quantum cryptography research is underway, but no upgrade has been deployed. The clock is running, even if no one knows exactly how fast.

sources.title: A simple explainer on what quantum computing actually is, and why it is terrifying for bitcoin

food.name: Miso-Glazed Aubergine (Nasu Dengaku)
food.description: Halved aubergines broiled until silky and caramelised under a sweet-savoury miso and mirin glaze
food.tip: Score the flesh in a crosshatch before glazing so the miso penetrates deeply rather than just sitting on the surface

exercise.name: Monday Reset: Loaded Carry Circuit
exercise.equipment: Two kettlebells or heavy shopping bags, a clear corridor or outdoor path
exercise.description: Alternate between farmer carries, suitcase carries, and overhead holds across 40-metre lengths to build grip strength, core stability, and posture after a weekend of likely sitting

learn.topic: The Ship of Theseus and Personal Identity Over Time
learn.description: The ancient paradox asks whether an object that has had all its components replaced remains fundamentally the same object — and philosophers from Hobbes to Parfit have weaponised it to interrogate whether you are the same person you were ten years ago
learn.why: Derek Parfit's modern extension in 'Reasons and Persons' leads to genuinely unsettling conclusions about survival, memory, and what you actually owe your future self — useful thinking for a Monday when you're setting the week's intentions
